draft.introduction: The “ask, seek, knock” passage from the Sermon on the Mount. Jesus promises that persistent prayer will be answered, grounding this promise in the analogy of a father’s generosity to his children.

translation.text: Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you. For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds. Or which one of you, if his son asks him for bread, will give him a stone? If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him!
translation.original_text: αἰτεῖτε καὶ δοθήσεται ὑμῖν ζητεῖτε καὶ εὑρήσετε κρούετε καὶ ἀνοιγήσεται ὑμῖν

translator_notes.note: The three imperatives—αἰτεῖτε, ζητεῖτε, κρούετε—are present tense, implying continuous action: “keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking.” The Fathers read this as an invitation to the progressive deepening of prayer life.
